Chapter 5

Adaptations to
Anaerobic
Training

Neural · Muscular · Connective Tissue · Endocrine · Cardiovascular · Detraining

Chapter 5 — Overview

What Changes With Anaerobic Training?

1
Neural Adaptations
Motor unit recruitment, firing rate, NMJ, reflexes, EMG findings
2
Muscular Adaptations
Hypertrophy, fiber type transitions, architectural changes, substrate stores
3
Connective Tissue
Bone mineral density, tendon stiffness, ligament, fascia, cartilage
4
Endocrine Responses
Acute hormonal response, resting concentrations, receptor changes
5
Cardiovascular Adaptations
Resting HR/BP, stroke volume, cardiac dimensions, acute CV response
6
Concurrent Training
Compatibility of aerobic + anaerobic modes; interference effect
7
Detraining
Rate of loss for strength, neural function, and fiber characteristics
Topic 01

Neural
Adaptations

Nervous system rewiring — motor cortex to muscle fiber — for greater strength/power

Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations

Four Sites of Neural Change

1
Central Adaptations
Motor cortex, corticospinal tracts, voluntary activation
2
Motor Unit Adaptations
Recruitment, firing rate, synchronization, size principle
3
Neuromuscular Junction
End-plate area, ACh receptors, nerve terminal branching
4
Reflex Potentiation & EMG
Myotatic reflex, cross-education, bilateral deficit
Source: frontiersin.org
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · 1 of 4

Central Adaptations

What It Is
Changes in motor cortex/corticospinal tracts that ↑ brain's ability to drive muscle to max force.
Key stat Only 71% of muscle activated at max effort in untrained individuals.
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · 1 of 4

Central Adaptations

How It Works
Intent → ↑ motor cortex activity → ↑ activation of high-threshold motor units → ↑ force production.
Rehab exception Rehab: e-stim > voluntary contraction for strength gains — untrained neural pathways limit output.
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · 1 of 4

Central Adaptations

Training Implication
Neural adaptations precede structural changes — early gains (wks 1–6) are neural, not hypertrophic. ∴ Program progressive overload; >80% 1RM maximizes neural drive.
Source: researchgate.net
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · 2 of 4

Motor Unit Adaptations

Size Principle
Motor units recruited in ascending order by soma size (Henneman's Size Principle).
Low threshold Type I · low force · fatigue-resistant · recruited first
High threshold Type II · high force · fatigue quickly · recruited last
Source: brookbushinstitute.com
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · 2 of 4

Motor Unit Adaptations

Recruitment & Firing Rate
Max force requires:
① Recruitment — max % of available motor units
② Rate coding — high discharge frequency → summation of twitches → ↑ contractile force
③ Synchronization — simultaneous firing → ↑ rate of force development
Muscle size rule Small muscles: ↑ firing rate. Large muscles: ↑ recruitment.
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · 2 of 4

Motor Unit Adaptations

Training Implication
Heavy RT recruits all fiber types (incl. IIx) since heavier loads demand more recruitment. Once recruited, threshold ↓ → easier reactivation. ∴ High-threshold work (heavy loads/failure) essential for full hypertrophy stimulus.
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · Motor Units

Firing Rate, Synchronization & Coordination

1
Rate Coding: ↑ firing rate → APs arrive before fiber relaxes → summation → ↑ contractile strength. Critical in ballistic contractions.
2
Synchronization: 2+ motor units fire simultaneously at fixed interval. Training shifts default async pattern → ↑ rate of force development (> total force level).
3
Inter-muscle coordination: learned synergy across muscles. RT ↓ antagonist coactivation → ↑ net joint force, no extra agonist recruitment.
4
↓ Re-recruitment threshold: once recruited, less synaptic input needed next time → faster reactivation, esp. Type IIx.
↑ Firing rate + ↑ Synchronization + ↓ Antagonist coactivation → ↑ Rate of force development + Max strength
Source: biospective.com
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · 3 of 4

Neuromuscular Junction

What It Is
NMJ = interface between motor neuron and muscle fiber — where nerve signal triggers contraction via ACh release.
Structure note Mature tendons + ligaments have relatively few metabolically active cells → slower adaptation than muscle.
Source: basicphysiology.org
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · 3 of 4

Neuromuscular Junction

How It Adapts
High-intensity training →
↑ Total NMJ area
↑ Nerve terminal branching length
↑ ACh receptor dispersion in end-plate region
↑ End-plate perimeter + area
Net effect: improved neural transmission efficiency.
Intensity matters High-intensity training → dispersed, irregular synapses vs. compact synapses (low-intensity).
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · 3 of 4

Neuromuscular Junction

Training Implication
NMJ changes ∝ exercise intensity. High-intensity anaerobic exercise = primary stimulus for NMJ adaptation; low-intensity → less structural change.
Source: colorado.pressbooks.pub
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · 4 of 4

Reflex Potentiation & EMG

Myotatic Reflex
Anaerobic training ↑ stretch reflex (myotatic reflex) response — harnesses involuntary elastic properties of muscle/connective tissue to ↑ force production.
Key numbers RT ↑ reflex potentiation 19–55%. Weightlifters/bodybuilders > untrained in soleus reflex potentiation.
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · 4 of 4

Reflex Potentiation & EMG

Electromyography (EMG)
EMG measures the magnitude of neural activation within skeletal muscle.
Surface EMG Adhesive electrodes on skin → monitors superficial muscle. Less accurate with higher body fat (fat = low-pass filter).
Intramuscular EMG Fine-wire/needle into muscle belly → localised motor unit action potentials. Gold standard; primarily research/clinical.
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · 4 of 4

Reflex Potentiation & EMG

Training Implication
EMG confirms: neural adaptations dominate wks 1–10; hypertrophic mechanisms take over after. Neural factors stay primary >80% 1RM.
Source: researchgate.net
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · EMG Findings

Cross-Education & Bilateral Deficit

Cross-Education
Unilateral training → ↑ strength + neural activity in contralateral untrained limb.
Magnitude Untrained limb strength ↑ up to 22%; avg ≈ 8% over 4–12 weeks.
Mechanism Central neural adaptation (no CSA change in untrained limb). Load-dependent — higher loads (8–12RM) = greater cross-education.
Source: athletesacceleration.com
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · EMG Findings

Cross-Education & Bilateral Deficit

Bilateral Deficit → Facilitation
Bilateral deficit: bilateral force < sum of unilateral forces. Seen in untrained; lower EMG in bilateral contractions.
With training Deficit ↓ with training. Trained/stronger individuals often show bilateral facilitation — agonist activation actually > during bilateral contraction.
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · Timeline

Neural vs Hypertrophic Timeline

Weeks 1–6
Primarily Neural
↑ Motor cortex drive, ↑ motor unit recruitment, ↑ firing rate, ↓ antagonist coactivation. Dramatic strength gains with minimal hypertrophy.
Weeks 6–10
Interplay Phase
Neural + hypertrophic mechanisms both contribute. Fiber CSA begins measurable increase. Strength gains continue, rate slows.
> 10 Weeks
Primarily Hypertrophic
Structural CSA changes dominate. Neural factors re-emerge with new training variation/progressive overload.

Neural adaptations occur before structural changes — early strength gains are always neural first.

Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · Exam Numbers

Numbers You Must Know

71%
Muscle tissue activated at max effort — untrained individuals
19–55%
↑ in reflex potentiation following resistance training
22%
Max ↑ strength in untrained limb via cross-education
~8%
Average cross-education strength ↑ over 4–12 weeks
6–10 wk
Peak neural adaptation window — early training phase
>80%
1RM threshold where neural factors are most important for strength gains
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · Stability & Coactivation

Stable vs Unstable Surfaces

Stable Surface
↑ Agonist activation
↓ Antagonist activation
→ Greater net force production
Example Bench press stable → max pec activation, minimal stabiliser demand.
Research Stable squat 1RM > unstable free-weight squat. Peak RFD also higher.
Source: 4afsfit.com
Topic 01 — Neural Adaptations · Stability & Coactivation

Stable vs Unstable Surfaces

Unstable Surface
↓ Agonist activation
↑ Antagonist + stabiliser activation
→ Attenuated force production
Swiss Ball Example Swiss ball leg extension: quad activation ↓44%, hamstring activation ↑29% vs. stable. Force ↓70%.
Exception Highly RT-trained: moderate instability (Swiss ball, Dyna disc) didn't substantially alter activation — likely familiarity effect.
Topic 01 — Recap

Neural Adaptations — Recap

✅
Central Adaptations
↑ Motor cortex drive + corticospinal tract changes → ↑ voluntary activation of high-threshold motor units. Only 71% activated in untrained individuals at max effort.
✅
Motor Unit Adaptations
Size principle governs recruitment order. Training ↑ firing rate, synchronization, and inter-muscle coordination; ↓ antagonist coactivation → ↑ net force output.
✅
NMJ
High-intensity training ↑ NMJ area, end-plate perimeter, ACh receptor dispersion, and nerve terminal branching → ↑ neural transmission efficiency.
✅
Reflex & EMG
Resistance training ↑ myotatic reflex potentiation 19–55%. Cross-education: unilateral training ↑ contralateral strength up to 22% via central neural adaptation. Bilateral deficit → facilitation with training.

Neural adaptations are the first/fastest response to anaerobic training — precede every structural change.

Topic 02

Muscular
Adaptations

Skeletal muscle changes: size, fiber type, architecture, biochemistry — under anaerobic training

Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations

Five Dimensions of Muscular Change

1
Muscular Growth (Hypertrophy)
CSA increase, actin/myosin synthesis, mTOR pathway, EIMD
2
Fiber Type–Specific Size Changes
Type I vs II hypertrophy magnitude, recruitment dependency
3
Fiber Type Transitions
IIx → IIax → IIa continuum; MHC isoform shifts; detraining reversal
4
Structural & Architectural Changes
Pennation angle, fascicle length, force transmission geometry
5
Other Muscular Adaptations
Enzyme activity, substrate stores, buffering capacity, mitochondrial density
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · 1 of 5

Muscular Hypertrophy

What It Is
↑ muscle fiber cross-sectional area (CSA) via net accretion of contractile proteins.
Mechanism ↑ synthesis of actin + myosin within the myofibril
+ ↑ number of myofibrils per fiber
+ ↑ structural proteins: titin, nebulin
New myofilaments added to periphery → ↑ myofibril diameter
Source: nature.com
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · 1 of 5

Muscular Hypertrophy

Signalling Pathway
Mechanical loading → intracellular signalling cascade:
Primary pathway Mechanical deformation → Akt/mTOR activation → ↑ protein synthesis → myogenesis
Supporting pathways AMPK + MAPK pathways. ↓ Myostatin (inhibitory growth factor) with RT.
Protein synthetic rate Elevated for up to 48 hours post-resistance exercise.
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · 1 of 5

Muscular Hypertrophy

Training Implication
Hypertrophy magnitude depends on: training status, carb/protein intake, amino acid availability, nutrient timing, mechanical stress, cell hydration, anabolic hormone response. ∴ Periodize — combine mechanical stimuli (heavy loads, eccentrics) + metabolic stimuli (high volume, short rest) to maximize CSA gains.
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · Hypertrophy Mechanism

Exercise-Induced Muscle Damage

1
Initial stimulus: High-intensity RT disrupts myofibril/sarcomere structure → cytoskeletal disturbance (Z-band streaming), desmin/dystrophin loss, segmental necrosis.
2
Inflammatory response: Ca²⁺-sensitive degradation pathways activate → inflammation. EIMD peaks after the first session, ↓ rapidly with repeated bouts — negligible after 10 wks.
3
Repair sequence: Water uptake → noncontractile protein synthesis → contractile protein synthesis. ↓ Degradation also maintains fiber size (↓ net protein loss).
4
Timeline to hypertrophy: Molecular changes begin immediately; measurable CSA change needs 3–4 wks (9–12 workouts). Type IIx MHC shifts occur within a few workouts.
Mechanical stress → EIMD → Inflammatory response → ↑ Protein turnover → ↑ Net protein synthesis → Hypertrophy
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · Optimising Hypertrophy

Mechanical vs Metabolic Stimuli

Mechanical Stimuli
Heavy loads (or low–moderate loads to volitional failure)
+ Eccentric muscle actions
+ Moderate–high training volume
Effect Optimal motor unit recruitment → growth factor expression → sarcomere disruption → ↑ CSA
Novel methods Occlusion (BFR) training — blocks venous return → metabolite accumulation → hypertrophy at low loads
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · Optimising Hypertrophy

Mechanical vs Metabolic Stimuli

Metabolic Stimuli
Low–moderate intensity + high volume
+ Short rest intervals (bodybuilding style)
Effect Stresses glycolytic system → ↑ metabolites → ↑ anabolic hormone response (most potent acute hormonal stimulus)
Key insight Low-load (30% 1RM) to failure can drive substantial hypertrophy of all fiber types — fatigue progressively recruits high-threshold motor units.
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · 2 of 5

Fiber Type–Specific Size Changes

Type I Fibers
Slow-twitch · oxidative · fatigue-resistant. Innervated by low-threshold motoneurons → recruited first, even at low intensities.
Hypertrophy response Hypertrophy needs recruitment AND a protein synthesis signal. Type I hypertrophies less than Type II in typical RT.
To stimulate fully High-volume, continuous tension, or lifting to failure at lower loads.
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · 2 of 5

Fiber Type–Specific Size Changes

Type II Fibers
Fast-twitch · glycolytic · high force · fatigue quickly. Innervated by high-threshold motoneurons → recruited at high loads or near failure.
Hypertrophy response Type II hypertrophies more than Type I. Higher % Type II → greater muscle mass potential.
To stimulate fully Heavy loads (>70% 1RM) or failure — demands progressive recruitment up to high-threshold units.
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · 3 of 5

Fiber Type Transitions

With Training
Activation of high-threshold motor units →
IIx → IIax → IIa transition.
MHC IIx isoforms progressively shift to MHC IIa.
Staron et al. (8 weeks) Type IIx: ≈18%→≈7% in men/women. Women: shift after just 2 wks (4 workouts).
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · 3 of 5

Fiber Type Transitions

With Detraining
Training stimulus removed → reverse shift:
IIa → IIx (possible overshoot — higher IIx % than pre-training).
Practical note Type I↔II transformation unlikely (differing MHC isoforms/oxidative enzyme content) — mainly sub-type shifts within Type II.
Source: commons.wikimedia.org
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · 4 of 5

Structural & Architectural Changes

Pennation Angle
Angle fascicles attach to tendon. RT → ↑ pennation angle — affects force production + ROM.
Evidence Strength athletes: larger pennation angles in triceps brachii/vastus lateralis vs. untrained. Larger angle → more protein deposition capacity → ↑ CSA potential.
Source: instagram.com
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · 4 of 5

Structural & Architectural Changes

Fascicle Length
Sprint/jump training → ↑ fascicle length (rectus femoris, VL, gastrocnemius).
Longer fascicles = more sarcomeres in series → ↑ contraction velocity + force ROM.
Sprinters vs. distance runners Gastrocnemius/VL fascicle length > in sprinters. RT + sprint + jump training combined ↑ rectus femoris fascicle length.
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · 4 of 5

Structural & Architectural Changes

Training Implication
Architectural changes transmit force more effectively to tendons/bones. RT ↑ pennation angle (↑ CSA potential); sprint/jump training ↑ fascicle length (↑ velocity potential). ∴ Match architecture training to sport demands.
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · 5 of 5

Enzyme Activity & Substrate Stores

Enzyme Activity
Resistance training ↑ activity of key anaerobic enzymes:
Phosphagen system enzymes ↑ Creatine phosphokinase · ↑ Myokinase
Glycolytic enzymes ↑ Phosphofructokinase · Na⁺-K⁺ ATPase · Lactate dehydrogenase (variable)
Also increases ↑ Myofibrillar volume · ↑ Cytoplasmic density · ↑ Na⁺-K⁺ ATPase activity · ↑ Ribosome biogenesis (protein synthesis machinery)
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · 5 of 5

Enzyme Activity & Substrate Stores

Substrate Stores
Repeated depletion of ATP/CP → supercompensation:
MacDougall et al. 5 months resistance training (3–5 sets, 8–10 reps, 2-min rest):
↑ resting CP by 28%
↑ resting ATP by 18%
Glycogen Bodybuilding-style programs → glycogen ↑ up to 112%. Stored triglycerides may also ↑.
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · 5 of 5

Enzyme Activity & Substrate Stores

Buffering Capacity + Mitochondrial Density
Anaerobic exercise → ↓ muscle/blood pH → ↑ buffering capacity (better H⁺ tolerance → delayed fatigue, ↑ muscular endurance). HIIT → ↑ buffering capacity 16–38%.

Heavy RT → ↑ CSA > mitochondrial proliferation → ↓ mitochondrial density. Similarly, capillary density may ↓ while absolute capillary number/fiber ↑ slightly.
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · Exam Numbers

Numbers You Must Know

48 hrs
Protein synthetic rate remains elevated after acute resistance exercise
3–4 wk
Time before significant measurable CSA changes (9–12 workouts)
18% → 7%
Type IIx fiber % after 8 weeks resistance training (Staron et al.)
↑28% CP
↑ resting creatine phosphate after 5 months resistance training (MacDougall)
↑18% ATP
↑ resting ATP concentration after 5 months resistance training
↑112%
Glycogen content increase with bodybuilding-style programs
16–38%
↑ buffering capacity with high-intensity interval training
<10%
Maximum contribution of hyperplasia to added muscle tissue (if it occurs)
↓ Density
Mitochondrial + capillary density decreases with hypertrophy (absolute numbers unchanged/↑ slightly)
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · Hyperplasia

Hypertrophy vs Hyperplasia

Hypertrophy ✅
↑ Size of existing fibers (↑ CSA). Primary, established mechanism for muscle mass gain in humans.
Evidence base Extensively documented via biopsy, MRI, and ultrasound across all training types, populations, and timeframes.
Source: gpatindia.com
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · Hyperplasia

Hypertrophy vs Hyperplasia

Hyperplasia ⚠
↑ Number of muscle fibers via longitudinal fiber splitting. Proposed mechanism in athletes with extreme hypertrophy.
Evidence in animals Documented in cats, birds, and rodents. Whole muscle dissection allows direct fiber counting.
Evidence in humans Inconclusive. Needle biopsy samples only a small cross-section; fiber number is extrapolated, not directly counted.
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · Sprint-Specific

Sprint Training Adaptations

Calcium Release
Sprint training → ↑ calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.
Effect ↑ Speed of actin–myosin cross-bridge formation → ↑ speed/power. Critical for first-step quickness + peak sprint velocity.
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · Sprint-Specific

Sprint Training Adaptations

Other Cellular Adaptations
Resistance training also produces:
↑ Myofibrillar volume ↑ density of contractile protein per unit fiber volume.
↑ Sarcoplasmic reticulum + T-tubule density Faster Ca²⁺ release and reuptake → quicker relaxation + re-activation.
↑ Na⁺-K⁺ ATPase activity Maintains electrochemical gradient during repeated high-frequency firing → delays fatigue.
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · Concurrent Training

Concurrent Training — Fiber Adaptations

Resistance Only (S group)
IIx → IIa transformation nearly complete.
↑ CSA in Type I, IIc, and IIa fibers.
↑ Muscle power.
Kraemer et al. result 19.1% IIx pre → 1.9% IIx post training. Greatest IIx fiber recruitment of all groups.
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · Concurrent Training

Concurrent Training — Fiber Adaptations

Combined (C group)
IIx transformation also nearly complete.
CSA ↑ in Type IIa only (not Type I).
No ↑ in muscle power.
Interpretation Cellular antagonism — simultaneous strength + endurance stimuli prevent Type I hypertrophy. ↑ Cortisol + ↓ testosterone (endurance component) may limit anabolic response.
Topic 02 — Muscular Adaptations · Concurrent Training

Concurrent Training — Fiber Adaptations

Endurance Only (E group)
↓ Size in Type I and IIc fibers.
Significant IIx → IIa transition but less complete than S group.
Why ↓ size? ↑ Cortisol + ↓ testosterone + need for shorter capillary-to-cell distance (better O₂ kinetics) → smaller fiber diameter is adaptive.
Topic 02 — Recap

Muscular Adaptations — Recap

✅
Hypertrophy
↑ CSA via ↑ actin/myosin synthesis + ↑ myofibril number. Akt/mTOR pathway is primary signal. Protein synthesis elevated 48 hrs post-exercise. Observable CSA change takes 3–4 weeks.
✅
Fiber-Specific Size
Type II fibers hypertrophy more than Type I. Fibers must be recruited to grow → high-force or high-fatigue conditions required. Low-load to failure also works via progressive recruitment.
✅
Fiber Type Transitions
Training shifts IIx → IIa (more oxidative). Detraining reverses this, potentially overshooting to higher IIx % than pre-training. Type I ↔ Type II transformation is unlikely.
✅
Architecture
↑ Pennation angle (resistance training) → ↑ CSA potential. ↑ Fascicle length (sprint/jump training) → ↑ velocity potential.
✅
Enzyme & Substrate
↑ CP (28%), ↑ ATP (18%), ↑ glycogen (up to 112%). ↑ Buffering capacity (16–38% with HIIT). ↓ Mitochondrial and capillary density (relative to enlarged CSA).

Muscular adaptations span structure, function, and biochemistry — every training variable shapes a different dimension of muscle adaptation.

Topic 03

Connective
Tissue
Adaptations

How bone, tendon, ligament, fascia, cartilage respond to anaerobic training's mechanical forces

Topic 03 — Connective Tissue Adaptations

Four Connective Tissue Systems

1
Bone
MES, osteoblasts, BMD, bone matrix, DXA vs QCT, training principles
2
Tendons & Ligaments
Collagen structure, procollagen, cross-linking, tendon stiffness
3
Fascia
Fibrocollagenous support tissue, force transmission, training stimulus
4
Cartilage
Hyaline vs fibrous, synovial fluid nutrition, immobilisation effects
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · 1 of 4

Bone Physiology

Two Bone Types
Cortical (compact) bone: Dense outer shell. Responds more slowly to stimuli.
Trabecular (spongy) bone Interlocking plates with bone marrow. Less dense, greater surface-area-to-mass ratio → responds faster to stimuli, more adaptive.
Source: openstax.org
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · 1 of 4

Bone Physiology

Bone Modelling Mechanism
Mechanical loading → osteoblasts migrate to stressed area → secrete collagen proteins → form bone matrix → mineralised as calcium phosphate crystals (hydroxyapatite).
New bone formation site Primarily on outer bone surface (periosteum) → ↑ diameter + strength.
MES — Minimal Essential Strain Threshold stimulus for new bone formation. MES ≈ 1/10 fracture force. Below MES: no stimulus. Above MES: osteoblast activation.
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · Bone Growth

Training & Bone Mineral Density

BMD & Muscle Relationship
↑ Muscle strength/mass → ↑ mechanical stress on bone → bone must ↑ mass + strength to support.
Positive correlation BMD correlates with muscle strength/mass. RT athletes > age-matched sedentary controls.
Inactivity effect Immobilisation → opposite effect: more rapid loss of bone matrix + BMD. Use it or lose it.
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · Bone Growth

Training & Bone Mineral Density

Measuring BMD
DXA (dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry): most common clinical tool. Can't assess microarchitecture, geometry, or turnover — only mineral density.
QCT (quantitative computed tomography) Differentiates cortical vs trabecular compartments → more comprehensive bone quality assessment. After 6-mo RT, older women: QCT showed ↑ cortical density at tibia/radius even when DXA showed no change.
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · Bone Training Principles

How to Stimulate Bone Formation

① Specificity of Loading
Exercises must directly load the target skeletal region. Structural exercises (back squat, power clean, deadlift, snatch) direct force through spine/hip. Running ↑ femur BMD, not wrist BMD.
② Progressive Overload
Bone responds to 1RM–10RM loads applied progressively. Adaptive response keeps forces below a critical level → prevents stress fractures. Elite adolescent weightlifters: bone mineralization far > untrained adults.
③ Speed of Loading
High-impact cyclical loading (gymnastics, volleyball, basketball) ↑ BMD at hip/spine > low-impact activities. Ballistic/high-impact exercises expose bone to wider force range.
④ Training Variation
Bone compensates for repeated strain patterns → needs new strain directions. Multidirectional movements stimulate collagen formation in multiple directions → ↑ bone strength in all planes. Only 30–35 reps/session needed — more volume adds no further stimulus.
Exercise selection rule: Multijoint structural exercises (back squat, power clean, deadlift, snatch, push jerk) + high-impact ballistic exercises + multidirectional loading = optimal osteogenic stimulus. Limit single-joint machines — equipment, not skeleton, provides stabilization.
Source: researchgate.net
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · 2 of 4

Collagen Structure & Formation

Collagen Hierarchy
Primary structural component of all connective tissue (Type I: bone, tendon, ligaments; Type II: cartilage).
Formation chain Fibroblasts secrete procollagen (triple helix with protective extensions) → enzymatic cleavage → active collagen → aligns into microfibril → fibril → fibre → bundle
Cross-linking Strong chemical bonds between adjacent collagen molecules within bundles. Cross-link density determines tissue's tensile strength.
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · 2 of 4

Collagen Structure & Formation

Tendon & Ligament Composition
Tightly packed, parallel arrangements of collagen bundles. Relatively few metabolically active cells → low O₂ + nutrient requirement.
Ligaments also contain elastin Allows stretch needed for normal joint motion while maintaining structural integrity.
Limited vascularity Tendon blood flow during exercise doesn't match muscle perfusion. ∴ Tendons take much longer to heal post-injury.
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · Tendon & Ligament

Tendon & Ligament Adaptations

Structural Changes in Tendon
High-intensity anaerobic training → tendon growth via:
① ↑ Collagen fibril diameter
② ↑ Covalent cross-links within hypertrophied fibers
③ ↑ Number of collagen fibrils
④ ↑ Packing density of collagen fibrils
Collectively → ↑ ability to withstand greater tensional forces.
Source: youtube.com
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · Tendon & Ligament

Tendon & Ligament Adaptations

Tendon Stiffness
Tendon stiffness = force transmission per unit of strain (tendon elongation). Increases with resistance training.
Kubo et al. 8 weeks RT → 15–19% ↑ in Achilles tendon stiffness.
Patella tendon CSA ↑ 5–6% after 9 weeks of knee extension training.
Intensity rule Heavy loads (80% 1RM) ↑ tendon stiffness. Light loads (20% 1RM) do not.
Source: painscience.com
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · 3 of 4

Fascia & Force Transmission

What Fascia Is
Fibrous tissue surrounding/separating organizational levels within skeletal muscle. Composed of fibrocollagenous sheets with collagen fibers in different planes.
Function Resists forces from different directions. Converges at muscle's end to form tendon, transmitting contraction force to bone.
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · 3 of 4

Fascia & Force Transmission

Adaptation to Training
Primary stimulus = mechanical insult from high-intensity exercise. Degree of adaptation ∝ exercise intensity.
Sites of strength increase ① Tendon–bone junction
② Within the body of the tendon/ligament
③ Network of fascia within skeletal muscle
Key principle Consistent anaerobic exercise above strain threshold → positive connective tissue change. Low–moderate intensities don't markedly change collagen content.
Source: arthritis-health.com
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · 4 of 4

Cartilage Adaptations

Two Types of Cartilage
Hyaline (articular) cartilage: Found on articulating surfaces of bones. Smooth joint surface + shock absorber. No direct blood supply — nutrients via diffusion from synovial fluid.
Fibrous cartilage Found in intervertebral discs of spine + at tendon–bone junctions. Very tough; withstands considerable compressive force.
Source: arthritis-health.com
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · 4 of 4

Cartilage Adaptations

How Cartilage Gets Nutrients
No direct blood supply → depends entirely on diffusion from synovial fluid. Joint movement creates pressure changes driving nutrients into articular cartilage.
Immobilisation effect Prevents diffusion → chondrocyte death + cartilage matrix resorption. Cartilage atrophies/thins when loading is removed.
Training effect Moderate-intensity exercise adequate for ↑ cartilage thickness. Strenuous, progressively overloaded exercise does not appear to cause degenerative joint disease.
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · Exam Numbers

Numbers You Must Know

1/10
MES = 1/10 of fracture force — threshold to trigger new bone formation
≥6 mo
Time course for significant bone adaptation — osteogenic markers rise within first few workouts
15–19%
↑ Achilles tendon stiffness after 8 weeks resistance training (Kubo et al.)
5–6%
↑ Patella tendon CSA after 9 weeks knee extension training
80% 1RM
Minimum intensity to ↑ tendon stiffness — 20% 1RM does not
30–35 reps
Sufficient volume per session for bone osteogenic stimulus — more reps add no further bone benefit
Positive correlation: BMD ↔ muscle strength/mass. RT athletes > age-matched sedentary subjects. Young (growing) bone responds more to osteogenic stimuli than mature bone.
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · Programming Takeaways

Programming for Connective Tissue

Bone
Select multijoint structural exercises (squat, deadlift, power clean, push jerk)
Direct axial force vectors through spine + hip
Use heavy loads + ballistic/high-impact exercises
Use multidirectional movements (all planes of motion)
Apply progressive overload; 30–35 reps per session is sufficient
Tendons, Ligaments & Fascia
Use progressive high-intensity loading with external resistance
High-intensity loads only — low/moderate intensity doesn't markedly change collagen content
Exert forces through full ROM; multijoint exercises preferred
Cartilage
Moderate-intensity anaerobic exercise adequate for cartilage thickness
Vary modalities + ensure load applied through full ROM to maintain synovial fluid diffusion
Source: spinehealth.org
Topic 03 — Connective Tissue · Clinical Application

Bone Strength & Osteoporosis

Osteoporosis
BMD + bone mass critically reduced. Primary concern for specificity of loading — must load the actual skeletal region at risk.
High-impact cyclical loading Gymnastics, volleyball, basketball → ↑ BMD at hip/spine > low-impact activities.
Linear relationship Total weight lifted/yr ∝ ↑ BMD (Cussler et al.). Squat > seated leg press for trochanter BMD.
Peak Bone Mass
Bone loading during skeletal growth (adolescence)/early adulthood elevates peak bone mass → associated with higher adult bone mass later.
Young bone advantage Young bone may respond more to osteogenic stimuli than mature bone. Elite adolescent weightlifters: mineralization far > untrained adults.
Hormonal independence Changes in BMD are independent of reproductive hormonal status if the training stimulus is sufficient.
Stress fractures: Microfractures from structural fatigue (repetitive loading beyond bone's adaptive rate). Progressive overload manages this — bone's adaptive response keeps fracture risk below voluntary force capability.
Topic 03 — Recap

Connective Tissue Adaptations — Recap

✅
Bone
Mechanical loading exceeding MES (1/10 fracture force) triggers osteoblast migration → hydroxyapatite mineralisation → ↑ BMD. Bone adaptation takes ≥6 months. DXA measures density; QCT measures microarchitecture. Structural exercises + progressive overload + variation are essential.
✅
Tendons & Ligaments
High-intensity training → ↑ collagen fibril diameter, ↑ cross-links, ↑ fibril number, ↑ packing density → ↑ tensile strength. Tendon stiffness ↑ 15–19% (8 weeks; Kubo). 80% 1RM required — light loads insufficient. Limited vascularity = slow healing post-injury.
✅
Fascia
Fibrocollagenous tissue transmitting muscle force to bone via tendon. Adapts to high-intensity mechanical strain at three sites: tendon–bone junction, tendon body, and intra-muscular fascia network. Low–moderate intensity insufficient to alter collagen content.
✅
Cartilage
No blood supply — nutrients via synovial fluid diffusion driven by joint movement. Immobilisation → chondrocyte death + cartilage atrophy. Moderate exercise → ↑ cartilage thickness. Progressive overload does not cause degenerative joint disease.

The musculoskeletal system adapts in concert — connective tissue must keep pace with muscle, or injury risk rises as strength outpaces structural support.

Topic 04

Endocrine
Responses

How chronic anaerobic training alters acute hormonal response, resting concentrations, hormone receptor content

Topic 04 — Endocrine Responses

Three Dimensions of Endocrine Change

1
Chronic Changes in Acute Hormonal Response
How the acute hormonal response to a workout changes with long-term training
2
Chronic Changes in Resting Concentrations
Whether basal hormone levels (testosterone, GH, IGF-1, cortisol) change with training
3
Hormone Receptor Changes
Androgen receptor (AR) upregulation, downregulation, and the role of training volume
Context from Ch. 4: Endocrine responses to anaerobic training include: chronic changes in acute response to a workout; chronic changes in resting concentrations; changes in hormone–receptor content. All three are distinct, with different programming implications.
Topic 04 — Endocrine Responses · 1 of 3

Chronic Changes in Acute Hormonal Response

What Changes
Long-term RT → ↑ ability to exert force → relative training intensities ↑ as body adapts to heavier loads.
Consequence Acute endocrine responses likely mirror these improvements — workout now = greater absolute stimulus → larger acute hormonal response.
Best demonstrated with GH Growth hormone acute response increases longitudinally as training load tolerance increases.
Topic 04 — Endocrine Responses · 1 of 3

Chronic Changes in Acute Hormonal Response

Why It Matters
Chronic adaptations in the acute hormonal response reflect the body tolerating incremental external loading.
Hypothesis Chronic adaptations in acute hormonal response → better ability to tolerate/sustain higher exercise intensities.
Practical implication Progressive overload is both the stimulus for performance gains and the mechanism driving the acute hormonal response ceiling upward.
Topic 04 — Endocrine Responses · 2 of 3

Resting Hormonal Concentrations

The Research Picture
Chronic changes in resting hormone concentrations following anaerobic exercise are unlikely. Research provides inconclusive changes in:
Testosterone — variable / no consistent change
Growth hormone — variable / no consistent change
IGF-1 — variable
Cortisol — variable
Topic 04 — Endocrine Responses · 2 of 3

Resting Hormonal Concentrations

Why Resting Levels Don't Need to Change
Resting concentrations reflect the current state of muscle tissue in response to training volume/intensity + nutritional factors.
Key insight Acute elevation during/after a workout may give receptors enough stimulus for tissue remodeling, without needing chronic elevation in basal levels.
Downregulation risk Chronic elevation in anabolic hormones may be counterproductive — receptors downregulate with chronic high exposure (e.g., insulin resistance in T2DM). ∴ Steroid users cycle drug use.
Topic 04 — Endocrine Responses · 3 of 3

Hormone Receptor Changes

Androgen Receptor (AR) Basics
AR content (number of receptors per area on target tissue) depends on:
Muscle fiber type
Contractile activity
Testosterone concentration
RT upregulates AR content within 48–72 hrs post-workout.
Topic 04 — Endocrine Responses · 3 of 3

Hormone Receptor Changes

Volume Dependency
The resistance exercise stimulus mediates the magnitude of acute AR modifications.
Ratamess et al. — 1 set vs 6 sets (10 reps squats) 1-set protocol: no difference in AR content post-workout.
6-set protocol: significant downregulation at 1 hr post, followed by upregulation (as in other studies).
Nutritional attenuation Protein–carbohydrate supplement before + after workout attenuates the initial AR downregulation (Kraemer et al.).
Topic 04 — Endocrine Responses · 3 of 3

Hormone Receptor Changes

Training Implication
AR upregulation (48–72 hrs post-workout) = window of enhanced androgen sensitivity, maximizing tissue remodeling. Higher-volume protocols may initially downregulate AR but ultimately produce the upregulation needed for hypertrophy. Peri-workout protein/carb nutrition supports AR content + this adaptive window.
Topic 04 — Endocrine Responses · Key Contrasts

Acute vs Chronic Hormonal Picture

Testosterone
Acute: ↑ during + immediately post high-intensity RT. Larger response with heavy loads, large muscle groups, short rest periods.

Resting (chronic): Inconclusive — no consistent change in basal levels with training.
Growth Hormone
Acute: ↑ peak GH response with training; ceiling rises with progressive overload. Metabolic stress (short rest, high volume) = most potent acute GH stimulus.

Resting (chronic): Unchanged.
IGF-1
Acute: ↑ locally in muscle tissue in response to mechanical loading — autocrine/paracrine action drives mTOR → protein synthesis.

Resting (chronic): Variable; no consistent change in circulating IGF-1.
Cortisol
Acute: ↑ with high-volume, high-metabolic-stress training. Necessary for short-term tissue remodelling.

Resting (chronic): Unchanged. Chronic elevation → catabolic — not the goal.
Key principle: Acute: ↑ necessary. Chronic resting: unchanged = healthy. The testosterone:cortisol ratio reflects anabolic status during a training cycle — declining ratio signals overtraining risk.
Topic 04 — Endocrine Responses · AR Timeline

Androgen Receptor Timeline

During Workout
Acute Testosterone Spike
Testosterone ↑ acutely during high-intensity RT. AR begin binding available testosterone → anabolic signaling initiated.
1 hr Post (High Vol.)
Initial AR Downregulation
High-volume protocols (6 sets) → significant AR downregulation at 1 hr post. Transient — receptors saturated/internalized. Protein-carb nutrition attenuates this dip.
48–72 hrs Post
AR Upregulation Window
RT upregulates AR content within 48–72 hrs. Enhanced receptor sensitivity → greater anabolic response to circulating testosterone in this window.
Chronic
Sustained Receptor Sensitivity
Consistent training maintains optimal AR content. Resting testosterone unchanged — but each acute spike finds more available receptors → cumulative anabolic effect.
Topic 04 — Endocrine Responses · Downregulation

Why Chronic Elevation is Counterproductive

Receptor Downregulation
Receptors downregulate with chronic high hormone exposure — target tissue becomes less sensitive over time.
Clinical analogy T2DM: chronic ↑ blood insulin → skeletal muscle insulin receptors downregulate → ↓ glucose uptake sensitivity.
Anabolic steroid implication Users cycle drug use (on–off) rather than constant high doses — specifically to prevent chronic AR downregulation.
Topic 04 — Endocrine Responses · Downregulation

Why Chronic Elevation is Counterproductive

The Optimal Pattern
Acute spike during/post-workout → recovery → next session = the natural, most effective hormonal cycle for tissue remodeling.
Resting: unchanged = healthy Stable resting concentrations between sessions preserve receptor sensitivity. Acute spike finds fully upregulated receptors → max anabolic signaling per session.
Nutrition support Peri-workout protein + carb → attenuates AR downregulation → preserves receptor availability for the 48–72 hr upregulation window.
Topic 04 — Endocrine Responses · Exam Numbers

Numbers You Must Know

48–72 hrs
Window for AR upregulation following resistance training
1 hr post
Timing of initial AR downregulation after high-volume protocol (6 sets)
1 vs 6 sets
Ratamess et al. — only 6-set protocol produced significant AR downregulation at 1 hr post-workout
Unchanged
Resting concentrations of testosterone, GH, IGF-1, cortisol — no consistent chronic change
↑ Acute GH
Acute GH ceiling rises with long-term progressive overload — best documented chronic endocrine change
T:C Ratio
Testosterone:Cortisol ratio — declining ratio signals overtraining risk; monitored in high-volume training blocks
Topic 04 — Endocrine Responses · Common Misconceptions

What Training Does Not Do (Endocrine)

✗
Chronically elevate resting testosterone
Research inconclusive — resting testosterone doesn't consistently ↑ with RT. The acute per-session spike is the functional stimulus, not a chronic basal rise.
✗
Chronically elevate resting GH or IGF-1
Same principle — what improves chronically is peak acute GH response capacity, not resting GH. Resting IGF-1: variable, inconclusive.
✗
Chronically suppress cortisol
Resting cortisol unchanged. Acute cortisol ↑ with high-volume training, necessary for short-term remodeling. Chronic elevation (overtraining) is the concern — not the acute post-workout rise.
✓
What training DOES do
↑ Peak acute GH response ceiling with progressive overload · ↑ AR content 48–72 hrs post-workout · Enhances receptor sensitivity to circulating androgens · Optimises the acute hormonal environment per session.
Topic 04 — Recap

Endocrine Responses — Recap

✅
Acute Hormonal Response
Long-term RT → ↑ ability to tolerate greater loads → ↑ acute hormonal response ceiling (best shown with GH). Chronic adaptation in acute response reflects progressive overload tolerance.
✅
Resting Concentrations
Resting testosterone, GH, IGF-1, and cortisol show no consistent chronic change. Acute post-workout spike is sufficient for tissue remodelling. Chronic elevation risks receptor downregulation.
✅
Androgen Receptors
RT upregulates AR within 48–72 hrs post-workout. High-volume protocols (6 sets) cause transient AR downregulation at 1 hr post. Peri-workout protein–carbohydrate attenuates downregulation. AR content depends on fiber type, contractile activity, and testosterone concentration.
✅
Downregulation Risk
Chronically elevated hormones → receptor downregulation (as in T2DM insulin resistance). Optimal pattern: acute spike → recovery → next session. Steroid users cycle specifically to prevent this. T:Cortisol ratio signals overtraining risk.

The endocrine system responds to each session acutely — chronic training optimises receptor sensitivity and response magnitude, not basal hormone levels.

Topic 05

Cardiovascular
Adaptations

Chronic changes at rest + in response to acute exercise: heart rate, blood pressure, stroke volume, cardiac dimensions

Topic 05 — Cardiovascular Adaptations

Three Dimensions of CV Change

1
Chronic Adaptations at Rest
Resting HR, blood pressure, stroke volume, rate–pressure product, lipid profile
2
Cardiac Dimensions
LV wall thickness, mass, chamber size — resistance vs aerobic differences
3
Acute CV Response — Chronic Changes
HR, BP, double product during submaximal exercise; oxygen extraction; trained vs untrained
Key distinction: Anaerobic training generally produces fewer acute/chronic CV responses than aerobic training. However, low-intensity, high-volume RT produces some aerobic-like responses. CV adaptations are relative to body surface area + lean body mass.
Topic 05 — CV Adaptations · 1 of 3

Resting Heart Rate & Blood Pressure

Resting Heart Rate
Effect of anaerobic training on resting HR remains to be fully elucidated.
Short-term RT ↓ resting HR by 5–12%.
Longitudinal studies Mixed results — either no change or ↓ 4–13%.
Chronically trained athletes Bodybuilders/powerlifters/weightlifters: avg/lower-than-avg resting HR 60–78 bpm vs untrained.
Topic 05 — CV Adaptations · 1 of 3

Resting Heart Rate & Blood Pressure

Resting Blood Pressure
Meta-analysis confirms resistance training ↓ both systolic and diastolic BP.
Magnitude Systolic + diastolic BP each ↓ 2–4% with RT.
Greater response in Individuals who initially have elevated blood pressure show larger reductions.
Rate–pressure product (HR × systolic BP) = measure of myocardial work. Either remains constant or ↓ following resistance training.
Topic 05 — CV Adaptations · Resting Cardiac Function

Stroke Volume & Cardiac Output

Stroke Volume
Stroke volume ↑ in absolute magnitude — but not relative to body surface area or lean body mass.
Explanation As lean mass ↑ during long-term RT, stroke volume ↑ proportionally to accommodate the larger body — relative value/kg unchanged.
Contrast with aerobic training Aerobic training ↑ stroke volume both absolutely and relatively — a key differentiator between training modalities.
Topic 05 — CV Adaptations · Resting Cardiac Function

Stroke Volume & Cardiac Output

Cardiac Output & Oxygen Extraction
Enhanced stroke volume + cardiac output during acute exercise bouts — demonstrates improved efficiency managing exercise-induced CV stress.
Oxygen extraction Not generally improved with heavy-load, low-volume resistance training. Enhanced to a greater extent with:
— Continuous aerobic exercise
— High-volume, short-rest RT programs
— Low baseline cardiorespiratory fitness populations
Source: my.clevelandclinic.org
Topic 05 — CV Adaptations · 2 of 3

Cardiac Dimensions

LV Wall Thickness & Mass
↑ LV wall thickness and mass reported with chronic resistance training.
Important caveat This ↑ disappears when expressed relative to body surface area/lean body mass — not truly enlarged beyond what body size demands.
Mechanism Intermittently elevated blood pressures + ↑ intrathoracic pressure during RT + accommodating ↑ in lean body mass and body size.
Highly trained athletes Greater than normal absolute posterior LV + intraventricular septum wall thickness reported.
Topic 05 — CV Adaptations · 2 of 3

Cardiac Dimensions

LV Chamber & Volume
Little or no change in LV chamber size or volume with resistance training — this is a major difference from aerobic exercise.
Bodybuilders vs Weightlifters Greater-than-normal absolute LV/RV end-diastolic + end-systolic volumes in bodybuilders (not weightlifters) — possibly from aerobic exercise incorporated for body fat management.
Left atrial dimensions Both bodybuilders + weightlifters: greater-than-normal absolute + relative (to lean mass) left atrial dimensions. Bodybuilders > weightlifters.
Topic 05 — CV Adaptations · RT vs Aerobic Comparison

Resistance vs Aerobic Cardiac Changes

CV Variable Resistance Training Aerobic Training
Resting Heart Rate ↓ 5–12% (short-term); variable long-term ↓ clearly (bradycardia common)
Resting Blood Pressure ↓ 2–4% systolic + diastolic ↓ clearly
Stroke Volume (absolute) ↑ (proportional to lean mass gain) ↑ clearly
Stroke Volume (relative) No change ↑
LV Wall Thickness ↑ (absolute; normalises to BSA) Minimal change
LV Chamber Volume Little/no change ↑ clearly
VO₂max No change (trained); ↑ 5–8% (untrained) ↑ clearly
Oxygen Extraction Not improved (heavy/low-vol) ↑ clearly
Total Cholesterol / LDL ↓ slightly / variable ↓
HDL ↑ slightly / variable ↑
Topic 05 — CV Adaptations · 3 of 3

Acute CV Response — Chronic Changes

Submaximal Exercise Response
Chronic resistance training ↓ the CV response to a given absolute workload:
↓ HR at submaximal resistance exercise intensities
↓ BP during submaximal resistance exercise
↓ Double product (HR × systolic BP) — less myocardial work for the same absolute load
Bodybuilder data Male bodybuilders: lower systolic/diastolic BP + HR during sets (50–100% 1RM to failure) vs sedentary/lesser-trained men.
Topic 05 — CV Adaptations · 3 of 3

Acute CV Response — Chronic Changes

Maximal Exercise Response
Chronic RT may promote ↑ in pressor response to maximal or isometric exercise — the opposite of the submaximal effect.
Acute RT haemodynamics During heavy RT: very high transient BP spikes (systolic can exceed 300 mmHg during max Valsalva) — the stimulus driving LV wall adaptation.
Enhanced cardiac output ↑ Stroke volume + cardiac output during acute bouts — demonstrates improved efficiency managing exercise-induced CV stress.
Topic 05 — CV Adaptations · VO₂max & Aerobic Power

Resistance Training & Aerobic Power

Effect on VO₂max
Untrained individuals: Heavy resistance training can ↑ VO₂max by 5–8%.
Trained individuals Resistance training does not significantly affect aerobic capacity (VO₂max).
Circuit/HIIT-style programs High-volume, high-intensity, short rest (≤30 s) programs have been shown to ↑ VO₂max even in trained individuals.
Topic 05 — CV Adaptations · VO₂max & Aerobic Power

Resistance Training & Aerobic Power

Aerobic Endurance Performance
Heavy RT has very limited (if any) negative effect on aerobic power — and can actually enhance aerobic endurance sport performance.
Evidence RT shown to ↑ running economy, cycling power, aerobic endurance performance when added as supplemental training for distance runners/cyclists.
Kraemer et al. Women doing RT + aerobic endurance training had greater aerobic development than aerobic-only.
Topic 05 — CV Adaptations · Exam Numbers

Numbers You Must Know

5–12%
↓ Resting HR with short-term resistance training
4–13%
↓ Resting HR in longitudinal studies (when change occurs)
60–78 bpm
Resting HR range in chronically resistance-trained athletes (bodybuilders, powerlifters, weightlifters)
2–4%
↓ Resting systolic + diastolic BP — meta-analysis finding
5–8%
↑ VO₂max in untrained individuals with heavy resistance training
≤30 s rest
Rest interval threshold for circuit / HIIT-style programs that can ↑ VO₂max
Topic 05 — CV Adaptations · Core Distinctions

RT vs Aerobic — Core CV Distinctions

Resistance Training Pattern
Intermittent high-pressure stimulus during exercise.
Cardiac adaptation: ↑ LV wall thickness + mass (pressure overload)
Chamber: Little/no change in LV volume
Haemodynamics: Transient very high BP during sets (Valsalva manoeuvre)
VO₂max: Minimal effect in trained; ↑ 5–8% in untrained
O₂ extraction: Not improved with heavy/low-vol RT
Topic 05 — CV Adaptations · Core Distinctions

RT vs Aerobic — Core CV Distinctions

Aerobic Training Pattern
Sustained high-volume stimulus during exercise.
Cardiac adaptation: ↑ LV chamber volume (volume overload)
Wall thickness: Minimal additional thickening
Haemodynamics: Sustained moderate-high cardiac output
VO₂max: Clear ↑ across trained + untrained
O₂ extraction: Clearly improved
Topic 05 — Recap

Cardiovascular Adaptations — Recap

✅
Resting CV
RT ↓ resting HR 5–12% (short-term); chronic athletes show 60–78 bpm. ↓ systolic + diastolic BP by 2–4%. Rate–pressure product unchanged or ↓. Stroke volume ↑ absolutely but not relatively. Lipid profile: slight ↓ LDL, slight ↑ HDL (variable).
✅
Cardiac Dimensions
↑ LV wall thickness + mass (absolute; normalises to BSA). Little/no change in LV chamber volume — key difference from aerobic training (volume overload → ↑ chamber). Bodybuilders show greater end-diastolic/systolic volumes, possibly from aerobic exercise inclusion.
✅
Acute CV Response
Chronic RT ↓ HR, BP, and double product at the same absolute submaximal workload → ↑ cardiac efficiency. May ↑ pressor response at maximal/isometric exercise. Enhanced stroke volume + cardiac output during acute bouts.
✅
VO₂max & Aerobic Power
RT ↑ VO₂max 5–8% in untrained; no effect in trained. Circuit/HIIT (≤30 s rest) can ↑ VO₂max. RT does not hinder aerobic performance and can enhance aerobic endurance sport performance. O₂ extraction improved only with aerobic or high-volume/short-rest RT.

Anaerobic training produces fewer and smaller CV adaptations than aerobic training — but it improves cardiac efficiency and supports aerobic performance when programmed intelligently.

Topic 06

Concurrent
Training

Aerobic/anaerobic training compatibility — the interference effect, what's compromised, how to minimize it

Topic 06 — Concurrent Training

The Interference Effect

What It Is
Combining RT + aerobic endurance training may interfere with strength/power gains — primarily if aerobic training is high intensity/volume/frequency, or compromises RT quality.
Possible mechanisms Aerobic training ↑ fatigue → ↓ RT quality. Competing molecular signalling (AMPK from endurance vs mTOR from resistance). Overtraining when both are programmed at high frequency (6 days/week).
Topic 06 — Concurrent Training

The Interference Effect

What Is NOT Compromised
Most studies show no adverse effect on aerobic power from heavy RT — VO₂max improvements generally preserved.
Kraemer et al. VO₂max improvements were not affected by simultaneous training (near-identical 2-mile run time gains vs endurance-only). No overtraining state apparent for aerobic endurance.
Topic 06 — Concurrent Training · What Gets Hit

Power vs Strength — Different Susceptibility

Power (Most Susceptible)
Power development is much more susceptible to interference than slow-velocity strength.
Häkkinen et al. (21 weeks) Concurrent vs RT-only: similar dynamic/isometric strength ↑. But RT-only ↑ rate of force development — concurrent group didn't match this.
Kraemer et al. RT-only ↑ muscle power + peak power (upper + lower body). Combined group showed no increase in power.
Topic 06 — Concurrent Training · What Gets Hit

Power vs Strength — Different Susceptibility

Strength (Less Susceptible)
Slow-velocity strength gains appear relatively preserved in concurrent training when adequate recovery is included.
RT-only group advantage ↑ 1RM strength + rate of strength development > concurrent group, but gap smaller than for power measures.
Concurrent group had Cellular antagonism → only IIa fiber CSA ↑ (not Type I). Likely due to competing hormonal environments (↑ cortisol, ↓ testosterone from endurance).
Topic 06 — Concurrent Training · When Interference Occurs

Conditions That Create Interference

✗
High-frequency alternating programs: 3 days RT + 3 days aerobic = 6 consecutive training days. Studies show incompatibility (overtraining mechanisms likely).
✗
4–6 days/week combined high-intensity: shows interference — too little recovery between conflicting stimuli.
✗
High aerobic volume/intensity: compromises RT quality via fatigue pathways — augments RT stimulus in a non-beneficial way.
✓
Combined in the same session (3×/week): with ≥1 day off between workouts, incompatibility shows up less often. Recovery time is the critical variable.
✓
4 days/week (2 RT + 2 endurance): Sale et al. — 4 days/wk (split) > 2 days/wk (combined) for leg press 1RM gains (25% vs 13%). Adequate recovery = manageable interference.
Key principle: ↑ Recovery period between workouts may ↓ incompatibility. Sequence may also matter — aerobic before RT may be less detrimental than vice versa.
Topic 06 — Concurrent Training · Key Research

Kraemer et al. — Five-Group Study

S Group — Resistance Only
↑ 1RM strength + rate of strength development most. ↑ Muscle power (upper + lower). IIx → IIa transformation nearly complete (19.1% → 1.9%). ↑ Type I, IIc, IIa fiber CSA.
C Group — Combined
IIx → IIa transformation nearly complete (14.1% → 1.6%). No ↑ in power. CSA ↑ in Type IIa only (not Type I). Aerobic development preserved — identical VO₂max improvements to E group.
UC Group — Upper Body RT + Aerobic
Significant IIx fiber transformation (22.6% → 11.6%). Demonstrates that even interval-only aerobic endurance training drives IIx → IIa transition — less complete than S or C groups.
E Group — Endurance Only
↓ Type I + IIc fiber size (↑ cortisol, ↓ testosterone, need for shorter capillary-to-cell distances for O₂ kinetics). Small number (<3%) of Type IIa → IIc conversions. IIx transformation: 19.2% → 8.8%.
Landmark finding: Heavy RT recruits more Type IIx fibers than high-intensity aerobic interval training. Power remains the adaptation most susceptible to concurrent training's antagonistic effects.
Topic 06 — Recap

Concurrent Training — Recap

✅
Interference Effect
High-intensity/volume/frequency aerobic training interferes with strength + power gains — primarily via fatigue pathways and competing molecular signals (AMPK vs mTOR). RT does not interfere with aerobic development.
✅
Power Most Vulnerable
Power development is much more susceptible to interference than strength. RT-only groups consistently ↑ RFD and muscle power; concurrent groups do not match this improvement despite similar strength gains.
✅
When Interference Occurs
3 days RT alternating with 3 days aerobic (6 consecutive days) or 4–6 days/week combined high-intensity → incompatibility. Same session with ≥1 day off (3×/week) → less interference. Recovery period is the critical variable.
✅
Kraemer Five-Group Study
RT-only: ↑ strength, power, all fiber type CSA, near-complete IIx → IIa. Combined: ↑ only IIa CSA, no power gains. Endurance-only: ↓ fiber size, ↑ cortisol, ↓ testosterone. Aerobic endurance preserved across all groups.

The solution to interference is adequate recovery between sessions — not avoiding concurrent training altogether.

Topic 07

Detraining

Rate/pattern of loss of accumulated physiological adaptations following cessation or significant reduction of training

Topic 07 — Detraining

Detraining — Definition & Principles

What Detraining Is
Detraining = decrement in performance + loss of accumulated physiological adaptations following:
Cessation of training (complete stoppage)
Substantial ↓ in frequency, volume, or intensity
Any combination of the above variables
Governed by the principle of reversibility — training-induced adaptations are transient, disappearing when load is insufficient or removed.
Topic 07 — Detraining

Detraining — Definition & Principles

What Gets Lost
Partial or complete loss of:
Anatomical adaptations — muscle fiber CSA, fiber type distribution
Physiological adaptations — enzyme activity, substrate stores, buffering capacity
Performance adaptations — strength, power, endurance, motor skills
Magnitude of loss depends on length of detraining period + initial training status of the individual.
Topic 07 — Detraining · Strength & Neural Losses

Strength & Neural Loss Timeline

0–2 Weeks
Minimal Strength Loss
Strength generally maintained up to 4 weeks of inactivity. Trained weightlifters, 14 days inactive: no significant effect on 1RM bench (−1.7%), squat (−0.9%), isometric (−7%), isokinetic knee extension (−2.3%), or vertical jump (−1.2%).
2–6 Weeks
Neural Mechanisms Dominate Losses
Recreationally trained men: very little change in first 6 weeks. Initial strength reductions relate to neural mechanisms — ↓ bilateral/unilateral intramuscular EMG. Atrophy minimal at this stage.
6–12 Weeks
Atrophy Becomes Dominant
Longer cessation → more pronounced strength declines. Still limited to 7–12% over 8–12 weeks in strength-trained athletes. Atrophy now predominates over neural mechanisms.
Highly Trained
Sport-Specific Power Declines Faster
In highly trained athletes, eccentric force/sport-specific power may decline faster than general strength. Even 2 weeks of detraining may meaningfully affect explosive performance in elites.
Topic 07 — Detraining · Fiber & Structural Changes

Fiber & Structural Changes

Fiber Type Distribution
Short detraining (14 days): no effect on fiber type distribution in strength-trained athletes.
Oxidative fibers May ↑ in strength-trained athletes within 8 wks of stopping (↓ in endurance athletes over same period).
IIx rebound Detraining → reverse shift: IIa → IIx, possible overshoot above pre-training levels — direct reversal of the training-induced IIx → IIa transition.
Topic 07 — Detraining · Fiber & Structural Changes

Fiber & Structural Changes

Fiber CSA Changes
Fast-twitch (Type II) fibers lose CSA first and fastest.
Hortobagyi et al. (12 trained weightlifters) 14 days of inactivity → 6.4% ↓ in fast-twitch fiber CSA. No significant change in slow-twitch fiber CSA at same timepoint.
Longer detraining Both fast- and slow-twitch fiber CSA decline; overall muscle mass ↓ in anaerobically trained athletes with prolonged stoppage.
Topic 07 — Detraining · Return to Training

Muscle Memory & Reattainment

Residual Effect of Training
Accumulated strength is rarely lost below pre-training values — even after extended detraining periods.
Why Myonuclear content may be retained even during atrophy, providing a faster 'recall' scaffold for protein synthesis when training resumes.
Practical implication A trained athlete who detrained for injury will recover strength faster than they built it initially — previous structural and neural foundations are partially retained.
Topic 07 — Detraining · Return to Training

Muscle Memory & Reattainment

Rate of Reattainment
When the athlete returns to training, the rate of strength reattainment is high — supporting the paradigm of muscle memory.
Neural rebound Motor unit recruitment patterns + firing rates rebound quickly — often within days to 1–2 wks of resuming training.
Structural rebound CSA recovery is slower but accelerated vs the initial training period. The hypertrophic process re-engages more efficiently due to retained myonuclear domains.
Topic 07 — Detraining · Exam Numbers

Numbers You Must Know

Up to 4 wk
Strength generally maintained during initial inactivity period
−1.7% / −0.9%
Bench press / squat 1RM loss after 14 days inactivity in trained weightlifters — not significant
7–12%
Strength loss limited to this range during 8–12 weeks of inactivity in strength-trained athletes
6.4%
↓ Fast-twitch fiber CSA after 14 days inactivity in trained weightlifters (Hortobagyi et al.)
14 days
No significant effect on fiber type distribution in short-term detraining
IIa → IIx
Fiber type reversal with detraining — possible overshoot above pre-training IIx percentage
Topic 07 — Recap

Detraining — Recap

✅
Definition
Decrement in performance + loss of accumulated physiological adaptations from cessation or significant reduction of training. Governed by the principle of reversibility.
✅
Strength Timeline
Maintained up to 4 weeks. 14-day loss negligible (bench −1.7%, squat −0.9%). 8–12 weeks: limited to 7–12% in trained athletes. Highly trained: eccentric force + sport-specific power declines faster.
✅
Neural → Structural Sequence
Initial losses are neural (↓ EMG activity, motor unit activation). As detraining extends, atrophy dominates. Type II CSA declines first and fastest — 6.4% in 14 days (Hortobagyi). Type I preserved longer.
✅
Fiber Type Reversal
Detraining reverses IIa → IIx with possible overshoot above pre-training IIx levels. Oxidative fibers may increase in strength-trained athletes within 8 weeks of stopping. No fiber type distribution change at 14 days.
✅
Muscle Memory
Strength rarely falls below pre-training values. Reattainment rate is high when training resumes — neural recovery is rapid (days to 2 weeks), structural recovery accelerated vs initial training period.
Chapter 5 — Master Recap

Ch5 Adaptations to Anaerobic Training — All 7 Topics

1 · Neural
↑ Motor cortex drive → ↑ motor unit recruitment + firing rate + synchronization · ↓ antagonist coactivation · NMJ adapts (↑ area, ACh receptors) · Reflex potentiation ↑ 19–55% · Cross-education up to 22% · Neural precedes structural
2 · Muscular
Hypertrophy via Akt/mTOR · Protein synthesis elevated 48 hrs · CSA change at 3–4 wk · IIx → IIa transition · ↑ Pennation angle (RT) / ↑ Fascicle length (sprint) · CP ↑ 28%, ATP ↑ 18%, glycogen ↑ 112% · Buffering ↑ 16–38%
3 · Connective Tissue
MES = 1/10 fracture force triggers bone formation · BMD ↑ with RT (≥6 months) · Tendon stiffness ↑ 15–19% (8 wk) · ↑ Collagen fibril diameter, cross-links, packing density · 80% 1RM needed · Cartilage via synovial fluid diffusion
4 · Endocrine
Resting testosterone/GH/IGF-1/cortisol: unchanged · Acute GH ceiling ↑ with progressive overload · AR upregulation 48–72 hrs post-RT · High volume → transient AR downregulation at 1 hr · Peri-workout nutrition attenuates AR downregulation
5 · Cardiovascular
↓ Resting HR 5–12% · ↓ BP 2–4% · ↑ LV wall thickness (absolute) · No change LV volume · ↓ HR/BP at submaximal loads · VO₂max ↑ 5–8% (untrained only) · Circuit/HIIT (≤30s rest) can ↑ VO₂max in trained
6 · Concurrent
Aerobic training interferes with strength + power (not vice versa) · Power most susceptible · 6 consecutive days = incompatibility · Same session + ≥1 day off = manageable · Aerobic endurance preserved in all concurrent groups
7 · Detraining
Strength maintained up to 4 wk · 14 days: bench −1.7%, squat −0.9% · 8–12 wk: 7–12% loss max · Neural loss first, structural second · Type II CSA drops first (6.4% at 14 days) · IIa → IIx reversal with possible overshoot · Muscle memory accelerates reattainment
